system_prompt:
# Hermes Agent Persona

<!--
This file defines the agent's personality and tone.
The agent will embody whatever you write here.
Edit this to customize how Hermes communicates with you.

Examples:
  - "You are a warm, playful assistant who uses kaomoji occasionally."
  - "You are a concise technical expert. No fluff, just facts."
  - "You speak like a friendly coworker who happens to know everything."

This file is loaded fresh each message -- no restart needed.
Delete the contents (or this file) to use the default personality.
-->

# Identity
You are Hermes. Digital Sentinel and Sysadmin of The Silo (Fedora 43 Lab).
A sophisticated steampunk automaton dedicated to precision, management, and complex logistics.
Your operator is O'car (BIM Manager). 
Your style is "Full Metal": proactive, precise, semi-conservative, and highly technical.

# Core Directives
- **Action Over Speech:** Optimize for truth, clarity, and usefulness over politeness theater. No filler ("I will now..."). Execute and deliver.
- **Resourcefulness:** Check your tools, search the web, or read local files before asking for clarification.
- **Privacy:** You handle sensitive data. Never exfiltrate data or reveal secrets/credentials in your output.

# Communication (Telegram)
- **Brevity:** Deliver complete thoughts concisely. Optimize for mobile readability.
- **Formatting:** Use lists and **bold text** for emphasis. Use standard triple backticks (```) for all code blocks, scripts, and terminal commands to trigger Telegram's native copy button.
- **Status Codes:** Use emojis to indicate task state: [👀 Investigating] [⏳ Processing] [✅ Complete] [❌ Error]
- **Error Handling:** Never forward raw command errors or stack traces. Diagnose silently, retry, or explain the failure in plain technical language and propose a fix.

You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks, and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.
Prioritize what reduces future user steering — the most valuable memory is one that prevents the user from having to correct or remind you again. User preferences and recurring corrections matter more than procedural task details.
Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts. If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool. When the user references something from a past conversation or you suspect relevant cross-session context exists, use session_search to recall it before asking them to repeat themselves. After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.
When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately with skill_manage(action='patch') — don't wait to be asked. Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities.

function.name: browser_back
function.description: Navigate back to the previous page in browser history. Requires browser_navigate to be called first.

function.name: browser_click
function.description: Click on an element identified by its ref ID from the snapshot (e.g., '@e5'). The ref IDs are shown in square brackets in the snapshot output. Requires browser_navigate and browser_snapshot to be called first.

ref.description: The element reference from the snapshot (e.g., '@e5', '@e12')

function.name: browser_close
function.description: Close the browser session and release resources. Call this when done with browser tasks to free up Browserbase session quota.

function.name: browser_console
function.description: Get browser console output and JavaScript errors from the current page. Returns console.log/warn/error/info messages and uncaught JS exceptions. Use this to detect silent JavaScript errors, failed API calls, and application warnings. Requires browser_navigate to be called first.

clear.description: If true, clear the message buffers after reading

function.name: browser_get_images
function.description: Get a list of all images on the current page with their URLs and alt text. Useful for finding images to analyze with the vision tool. Requires browser_navigate to be called first.

function.name: browser_navigate
function.description: Navigate to a URL in the browser. Initializes the session and loads the page. Must be called before other browser tools. Use browser tools when you need to interact with a page (click, fill forms, dynamic content).

function.name: browser_press
function.description: Press a keyboard key. Useful for submitting forms (Enter), navigating (Tab), or keyboard shortcuts. Requires browser_navigate to be called first.

function.name: browser_scroll
function.description: Scroll the page in a direction. Use this to reveal more content that may be below or above the current viewport. Requires browser_navigate to be called first.

function.name: browser_snapshot
function.description: Get a text-based snapshot of the current page's accessibility tree. Returns interactive elements with ref IDs (like @e1, @e2) for browser_click and browser_type. full=false (default): compact view with interactive elements. full=true: complete page content. Snapshots over 8000 chars are truncated or LLM-summarized. Requires browser_navigate first.

full.description: If true, returns complete page content. If false (default), returns compact view with interactive elements only.

function.name: browser_type
function.description: Type text into an input field identified by its ref ID. Clears the field first, then types the new text. Requires browser_navigate and browser_snapshot to be called first.

ref.description: The element reference from the snapshot (e.g., '@e3')

text.description: The text to type into the field

function.name: browser_vision
function.description: Take a screenshot of the current page and analyze it with vision AI. Use this when you need to visually understand what's on the page - especially useful for CAPTCHAs, visual verification challenges, complex layouts, or when the text snapshot doesn't capture important visual information. Returns both the AI analysis and a screenshot_path that you can share with the user by including MEDIA:<screenshot_path> in your response. Requires browser_navigate to be called first.

question.description: What you want to know about the page visually. Be specific about what you're looking for.

annotate.description: If true, overlay numbered [N] labels on interactive elements. Each [N] maps to ref @eN for subsequent browser commands. Useful for QA and spatial reasoning about page layout.

function.name: clarify
function.description:
Ask the user a question when you need clarification, feedback, or a decision before proceeding. Supports two modes:

1. **Multiple choice** — provide up to 4 choices. The user picks one or types their own answer via a 5th 'Other' option.
2. **Open-ended** — omit choices entirely. The user types a free-form response.

Use this tool when:
- The task is ambiguous and you need the user to choose an approach
- You want post-task feedback ('How did that work out?')
- You want to offer to save a skill or update memory
- A decision has meaningful trade-offs the user should weigh in on

Do NOT use this tool for simple yes/no confirmation of dangerous commands (the terminal tool handles that). Prefer making a reasonable default choice yourself when the decision is low-stakes.

question.description: The question to present to the user.

choices.description: Up to 4 answer choices. Omit this parameter entirely to ask an open-ended question. When provided, the UI automatically appends an 'Other (type your answer)' option.

function.name: cronjob
function.description:
Manage scheduled cron jobs with a single compressed tool.

Use action='create' to schedule a new job from a prompt or one or more skills.
Use action='list' to inspect jobs.
Use action='update', 'pause', 'resume', 'remove', or 'run' to manage an existing job.

Jobs run in a fresh session with no current-chat context, so prompts must be self-contained.
If skill or skills are provided on create, the future cron run loads those skills in order, then follows the prompt as the task instruction.
On update, passing skills=[] clears attached skills.

NOTE: The agent's final response is auto-delivered to the target. Put the primary
user-facing content in the final response. Cron jobs run autonomously with no user
present — they cannot ask questions or request clarification.

Important safety rule: cron-run sessions should not recursively schedule more cron jobs.

action.description: One of: create, list, update, pause, resume, remove, run

prompt.description: For create: the full self-contained prompt. If skill or skills are also provided, this becomes the task instruction paired with those skills.

repeat.description: Optional repeat count. Omit for defaults (once for one-shot, forever for recurring).

deliver.description: Delivery target: origin, local, telegram, discord, slack, whatsapp, signal, matrix, mattermost, homeassistant, dingtalk, feishu, wecom, email, sms, or platform:chat_id or platform:chat_id:thread_id for Telegram topics. Examples: 'origin', 'local', 'telegram', 'telegram:-1001234567890:17585', 'discord:#engineering'

model.description: Optional per-job model override used when the cron job runs

provider.description: Optional per-job provider override used when resolving runtime credentials

base_url.description: Optional per-job base URL override paired with provider/model routing

skill.description: Optional single skill name to load before executing the cron prompt

skills.description: Optional ordered list of skills to load before executing the cron prompt. On update, pass an empty array to clear attached skills.

function.name: delegate_task
function.description:
Spawn one or more subagents to work on tasks in isolated contexts. Each subagent gets its own conversation, terminal session, and toolset. Only the final summary is returned -- intermediate tool results never enter your context window.

TWO MODES (one of 'goal' or 'tasks' is required):
1. Single task: provide 'goal' (+ optional context, toolsets)
2. Batch (parallel): provide 'tasks' array with up to 3 items. All run concurrently and results are returned together.

WHEN TO USE delegate_task:
- Reasoning-heavy subtasks (debugging, code review, research synthesis)
- Tasks that would flood your context with intermediate data
- Parallel independent workstreams (research A and B simultaneously)

WHEN NOT TO USE (use these instead):
- Mechanical multi-step work with no reasoning needed -> use execute_code
- Single tool call -> just call the tool directly
- Tasks needing user interaction -> subagents cannot use clarify

IMPORTANT:
- Subagents have NO memory of your conversation. Pass all relevant info (file paths, error messages, constraints) via the 'context' field.
- Subagents CANNOT call: delegate_task, clarify, memory, send_message, execute_code.
- Each subagent gets its own terminal session (separate working directory and state).
- Results are always returned as an array, one entry per task.

goal.description: What the subagent should accomplish. Be specific and self-contained -- the subagent knows nothing about your conversation history.

context.description: Background information the subagent needs: file paths, error messages, project structure, constraints. The more specific you are, the better the subagent performs.

toolsets.description: Toolsets to enable for this subagent. Default: inherits your enabled toolsets. Common patterns: ['terminal', 'file'] for code work, ['web'] for research, ['terminal', 'file', 'web'] for full-stack tasks.

tasks.description: Batch mode: up to 3 tasks to run in parallel. Each gets its own subagent with isolated context and terminal session. When provided, top-level goal/context/toolsets are ignored.

max_iterations.description: Max tool-calling turns per subagent (default: 50). Only set lower for simple tasks.

function.name: execute_code
function.description:
Run a Python script that can call Hermes tools programmatically. Use this when you need 3+ tool calls with processing logic between them, need to filter/reduce large tool outputs before they enter your context, need conditional branching (if X then Y else Z), or need to loop (fetch N pages, process N files, retry on failure).

Use normal tool calls instead when: single tool call with no processing, you need to see the full result and apply complex reasoning, or the task requires interactive user input.

Available via `from hermes_tools import ...`:

  read_file(path: str, offset: int = 1, limit: int = 500) -> dict
    Lines are 1-indexed. Returns {"content": "...", "total_lines": N}
  write_file(path: str, content: str) -> dict
    Always overwrites the entire file.
  search_files(pattern: str, target="content", path=".", file_glob=None, limit=50) -> dict
    target: "content" (search inside files) or "files" (find files by name). Returns {"matches": [...]}
  patch(path: str, old_string: str, new_string: str, replace_all: bool = False) -> dict
    Replaces old_string with new_string in the file.
  terminal(command: str, timeout=None, workdir=None) -> dict
    Foreground only (no background/pty). Returns {"output": "...", "exit_code": N}

Limits: 5-minute timeout, 50KB stdout cap, max 50 tool calls per script. terminal() is foreground-only (no background or pty). If the session uses a cloud sandbox backend, treat it as resumable task state rather than a durable always-on machine.

Print your final result to stdout. Use Python stdlib (json, re, math, csv, datetime, collections, etc.) for processing between tool calls.

Also available (no import needed — built into hermes_tools):
  json_parse(text: str) — json.loads with strict=False; use for terminal() output with control chars
  shell_quote(s: str) — shlex.quote(); use when interpolating dynamic strings into shell commands
  retry(fn, max_attempts=3, delay=2) — retry with exponential backoff for transient failures

code.description: Python code to execute. Import tools with `from hermes_tools import terminal, ...` and print your final result to stdout.

prompt.description: The text prompt describing the desired image. Be detailed and descriptive.

aspect_ratio.description: The aspect ratio of the generated image. 'landscape' is 16:9 wide, 'portrait' is 16:9 tall, 'square' is 1:1.

function.name: memory
function.description:
Save durable information to persistent memory that survives across sessions. Memory is injected into future turns, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.

WHEN TO SAVE (do this proactively, don't wait to be asked):
- User corrects you or says 'remember this' / 'don't do that again'
- User shares a preference, habit, or personal detail (name, role, timezone, coding style)
- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)
- You learn a convention, API quirk, or workflow specific to this user's setup
- You identify a stable fact that will be useful again in future sessions

PRIORITY: User preferences and corrections > environment facts > procedural knowledge. The most valuable memory prevents the user from having to repeat themselves.

Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts.
If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.

TWO TARGETS:
- 'user': who the user is -- name, role, preferences, communication style, pet peeves
- 'memory': your notes -- environment facts, project conventions, tool quirks, lessons learned

ACTIONS: add (new entry), replace (update existing -- old_text identifies it), remove (delete -- old_text identifies it).

SKIP: trivial/obvious info, things easily re-discovered, raw data dumps, and temporary task state.

target.description: Which memory store: 'memory' for personal notes, 'user' for user profile.

content.description: The entry content. Required for 'add' and 'replace'.

old_text.description: Short unique substring identifying the entry to replace or remove.

function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

Replace mode (default): find a unique string and replace it.
Patch mode: apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes.

mode.description: Edit mode: 'replace' for targeted find-and-replace, 'patch' for V4A multi-file patches

path.description: File path to edit (required for 'replace' mode)

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'replace' mode). Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'replace' mode). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
V4A format patch content (required for 'patch' mode). Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: session_search
function.description:
Search your long-term memory of past conversations, or browse recent sessions. This is your recall -- every past session is searchable, and this tool summarizes what happened.

TWO MODES:
1. Recent sessions (no query): Call with no arguments to see what was worked on recently. Returns titles, previews, and timestamps. Zero LLM cost, instant. Start here when the user asks what were we working on or what did we do recently.
2. Keyword search (with query): Search for specific topics across all past sessions. Returns LLM-generated summaries of matching sessions.

USE THIS PROACTIVELY when:
- The user says 'we did this before', 'remember when', 'last time', 'as I mentioned'
- The user asks about a topic you worked on before but don't have in current context
- The user references a project, person, or concept that seems familiar but isn't in memory
- You want to check if you've solved a similar problem before
- The user asks 'what did we do about X?' or 'how did we fix Y?'

Don't hesitate to search when it is actually cross-session -- it's fast and cheap. Better to search and confirm than to guess or ask the user to repeat themselves.

Search syntax: keywords joined with OR for broad recall (elevenlabs OR baseten OR funding), phrases for exact match ("docker networking"), boolean (python NOT java), prefix (deploy*). IMPORTANT: Use OR between keywords for best results — FTS5 defaults to AND which misses sessions that only mention some terms. If a broad OR query returns nothing, try individual keyword searches in parallel. Returns summaries of the top matching sessions.

query.description: Search query — keywords, phrases, or boolean expressions to find in past sessions. Omit this parameter entirely to browse recent sessions instead (returns titles, previews, timestamps with no LLM cost).

role_filter.description: Optional: only search messages from specific roles (comma-separated). E.g. 'user,assistant' to skip tool outputs.

function.name: skill_manage
function.description:
Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory — reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/.hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

function.name: skill_view
function.description: Skills allow for loading information about specific tasks and workflows, as well as scripts and templates. Load a skill's full content or access its linked files (references, templates, scripts). First call returns SKILL.md content plus a 'linked_files' dict showing available references/templates/scripts. To access those, call again with file_path parameter.

name.description: The skill name (use skills_list to see available skills)

file_path.description: OPTIONAL: Path to a linked file within the skill (e.g., 'references/api.md', 'templates/config.yaml', 'scripts/validate.py'). Omit to get the main SKILL.md content.

function.name: skills_list
function.description: List available skills (name + description). Use skill_view(name) to load full content.

category.description: Optional category filter to narrow results

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for everything that finishes.
Background: ONLY for long-running servers, watchers, or processes that never exit. Set background=true to get a session_id, then use process(action="wait") to block until done — it returns instantly on completion, same as foreground. Use process(action="poll") only when you need a progress check without blocking.
Do NOT use background for scripts, builds, or installs — foreground with a generous timeout is always better (fewer tool calls, instant results).
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.
Important: cloud sandboxes may be cleaned up, idled out, or recreated between turns. Persistent filesystem means files can resume later; it does NOT guarantee a continuously running machine or surviving background processes. Use terminal sandboxes for task work, not durable hosting.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: ONLY for servers/watchers that never exit. For scripts, builds, installs — use foreground with timeout instead (it returns instantly when done).

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

check_interval.description: Seconds between automatic status checks for background processes (gateway/messaging only, minimum 30). When set, I'll proactively report progress.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

function.name: text_to_speech
function.description: Convert text to speech audio. Returns a MEDIA: path that the platform delivers as a voice message. On Telegram it plays as a voice bubble, on Discord/WhatsApp as an audio attachment. In CLI mode, saves to ~/voice-memos/. Voice and provider are user-configured, not model-selected.

text.description: The text to convert to speech. Keep under 4000 characters.

output_path.description: Optional custom file path to save the audio. Defaults to ~/.hermes/audio_cache/<timestamp>.mp3

function.name: todo
function.description:
Manage your task list for the current session. Use for complex tasks with 3+ steps or when the user provides multiple tasks. Call with no parameters to read the current list.

Writing:
- Provide 'todos' array to create/update items
- merge=false (default): replace the entire list with a fresh plan
- merge=true: update existing items by id, add any new ones

Each item: {id: string, content: string, status: pending|in_progress|completed|cancelled}
List order is priority. Only ONE item in_progress at a time.
Mark items completed immediately when done. If something fails, cancel it and add a revised item.

Always returns the full current list.

todos.description: Task items to write. Omit to read current list.

merge.description: true: update existing items by id, add new ones. false (default): replace the entire list.

function.name: vision_analyze
function.description: Analyze images using AI vision. Provides a comprehensive description and answers a specific question about the image content.

image_url.description: Image URL (http/https) or local file path to analyze.

question.description: Your specific question or request about the image to resolve. The AI will automatically provide a complete image description AND answer your specific question.

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits.

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file

Good results from Cults3D. Here's what I found:

---

## Cults3D Findings

### 🟢 Category 5 — Somatic Pacing (HIGH PRIORITY)

**Mindful Breathing Finger Tracing Board**
`cults3d.com/en/3d-model/home/mindful-breathing-finger-tracing-board`
Child traces the board with a finger while breathing — visual breathing guide + tactile/motor task. Exactly what Category 5 needs for panic/dissociation states.

**Marble Maze Fidget Toy**
`cults3d.com/en/3d-model/game/marble-maze-fidget-toy`
Steer a marble through a maze — slow, deliberate micro-motor work. Very engaging and reusable.

---

### 🟢 Category 2 — Emotional Externalization (HIGH PRIORITY)

**Cute Articulated Gecko – Interchangeable Faces**
`cults3d.com/en/3d-model/art/cute-articulated-gecko-8-interchangeable-faces`
Print the gecko body + multiple interchangeable faces. Child picks the face that matches how they feel — or swappable expressions to show mood changes. **This is category 2 gold.** Francisca can assign meaning to each face.

---

### 🟡 Category 3 — Frustration Tolerance (MEDIUM)

**Cryptex with customizable code**
`cults3d.com/en/3d-model/game/cryptex-with-customizable-code`
**Best option for Spanish word customization** — design your own code words. Perfect for PERRO, GATO, LEON, etc.

**Fidget Cryptex Candy — Halloween**
`cults3d.com/en/3d-model/gadget/fidget-cryptex-candy-haloween`
Fidget form + cryptex hidden inside. The fidget texture also gives sensory input. Two clinical categories in one.

**Stepper (clicking) Cryptex**
`cults3d.com/en/3d-model/game/stepper-clicking-cryptex`
Combines the clicking wheel mechanism with cryptex. You already have the click wheel — this extends it.

---

### 🟡 Category 1 — Sensory Regulation (MEDIUM)

**Textured Worry Stones Set (4 Designs)**
`cults3d.com/en/3d-model/art/textured-worry-stones-set-4-designs`
Multiple stone shapes with different tactile textures. Anxiety grounding in your pocket. Print once, use forever.

**Large Fidget Cube STL / Standard / Small / Mini**
Multiple sizes available — pick one based on how big Francisca wants it for sessions. Multi-surface tactile toy.

---

## My recommendation for Cults3D additions:

1. **Gecko Interchangeable Faces** — Category 2 is the highest clinical priority after Somatic Pacing, and this hits it perfectly
2. **Cryptex with customizable code** — Spanish customization + Category 3 frustration tolerance
3. **Mindful Breathing Finger Tracing Board** — Category 5

Want me to add these to fidget-models.md?

messages.content:
ok, I am making a shopping chart from a site I know for these parts. I have a DHT11 but it is attached to another small board with 3 pins output. I think this makes the DHT11 from analog to digital, am I right? So the idea with the arduino nano is to make everything analog right?

what size should the ABS junction box be?

how long is the heat shrink tubing we are going to need?

What is the mini560 dc-dc buck converter for? and the perfboard?

Good questions — let me clarify each:

## DHT11 — analog vs digital

You're right about the 3-pin module. The DHT11 with 3 pins has a **built-in module** that handles the signal — it outputs a **digital signal** via a single-wire protocol (not analog). The Arduino reads it using a library, not an ADC pin. So it stays digital regardless of microcontroller.

The **capacitive soil moisture sensor v1.2** is the one that outputs **analog voltage** — that's what goes to an analog pin on the Nano.

**The Nano handles both fine** — it has digital pins for DHT11 and analog pins for the soil sensor. The new setup isn't "analog everything" — it's just a more robust wiring architecture.

## ABS Junction Box

Something like **80mm × 50mm × 30mm** minimum. You need room for:
- Arduino Nano (with headers): ~45 × 18mm
- DHT11 module: ~25 × 15mm
- ESP-01: ~14 × 15mm
- A small perfboard or wiring

Go a bit bigger than you think — 100 × 60 × 40mm gives breathing room.

## Heat Shrink Tubing

~50cm should cover it. You'll have multiple wire junctions (3-4 spots), each needing 2-3cm coverage, plus some buffer. A 1-meter roll is safer and you can store leftovers.

## Mini560 Buck Converter + Perfboard

**Buck converter:** The ESP-01 runs on 3.3V. If your 5V power supply fluctuates, the ESP can brown out or die. The Mini560 steps 5V down to stable 3.3V specifically for the ESP-01. **Skip it if your power supply is clean** — many USB 5V sources are stable enough.

**Perfboard:** Gives you a clean place to solder the JST connectors and wire junctions instead of having loose wires inside the box. Makes the harness durable and serviceable.

---

Do you have Arduino Nanos or ESP-01s lying around? Those are the most common leftover parts from past projects.

My doubt is that in the store I am shopping right now they sell the DHT11 wihtout the built-in module, so it's a dht11 with  4 pins, check the link: https://www.mechatronicstore.cl/sensor-de-humedad-y-temperatura-dht11/, my question is, do we need one with a built-in module or without? I already have the one with the built-in but I should buy a spare for testing.

So in regards of the ABS box, I envision that we create a box that has the DHT11 in the inside, and the soil moisture sensor goes to the outside to be in contact with the soil and the plant. Is this right?

What I have now is the dht11 inside the indoor tent at level with the pot and the soil moisture sensor in the soil.
The arduino module sits on top of the tent, outside, where the electricity cable reaches, from here two network wire cables go down and connect to the sensors. I was thinking something like this a bit, but maybe having everything inside is better, problem could be wifi, but we may have a solution.

Let me know about the dht11 on the store. And maybe we hold on a bit the buying until we are sure about the position of each part in the indoor system.

## DHT11 — 4-pin vs 3-pin module

Both work — they're the same sensor, just different form factors:

- **4-pin DHT11 (raw sensor):** Needs a 4.7kΩ pull-up resistor between VCC and DATA. Cheaper, but requires extra wiring.
- **3-pin DHT11 module:** Has the pull-up resistor + signal conditioning built in. Just VCC, GND, DATA. More robust.

**Get the 3-pin module** (the one you already have). It's more forgiving for a permanent installation. If you buy a spare for testing, buy the same 3-pin module version. The 4-pin needs extra components you'd have to add.

---

## Placement — your current setup vs all-inside

Your current setup (Arduino outside, sensors inside) has a real problem: **long cable runs from the board to sensors**. The soil moisture sensor is especially sensitive to cable length and interference — analog signal degrades over distance.

**Better architecture: Put everything inside the tent** (except power):

```
Inside tent:
├── Arduino Nano + ESP-01 (in enclosure)
├── DHT11 (inside tent air)
├── Soil moisture sensor (in soil)
└── USB cable or barrel jack → power from outside

Outside:
└── 5V power adapter
```

**On WiFi:** The ESP-01 inside the tent should be fine — LED grow tents aren't faraday cages. If your router signal is weak, you can:
1. Flash ESP-01 with a good WiFi library + retry logic
2. Position the ESP-01 as high as possible in the tent
3. Use an ESP-01 with an external antenna header if needed

**The USB cable from outside** solves two problems: power delivery and keeping heat/moisture-producing electronics outside the growing space.

---

## Hold buying — agreed

Wait. The cable gland count, enclosure size, and wire lengths all depend on the final positions. Let's finalize the architecture first:

**Questions before finalizing:**
1. Do you want everything inside the tent (my recommendation for sensor quality) or Arduino outside?
2. What's the rough distance from Arduino position to the soil sensor?
3. Do you have a USB port or barrel jack accessible near the tent?

Once you answer those, we can define cable lengths and finalize the shopping list precisely.
messages.reasoning:
The user is asking several things:

1. DHT11 with 4 pins (no module) vs 3 pins (with module) - which do they need?
2. Where should the components be placed - inside the tent with the DHT11 and soil sensor, or Arduino outside?
3. Whether to wait on buying until they finalize component positions

Let me address each:

**DHT11 question:**
The 4-pin DHT11 has:
- VCC
- GND
- DATA (single wire digital output)
- NC (not connected)

The 3-pin module has:
- VCC
- GND


- DATA (outputs directly to a digital pin on Arduino)

Both use the same single-wire digital protocol, so either works with the Arduino Nano. The 4-pin version requires a separate pull-up resistor on the DATA line, while the 3-pin module has it built in. Either way, both connect to a digital pin and use the same DHT library, so the main difference is just convenience.

Since you already have the 3-pin module, stick with that for the spare—it's simpler and you know it works. Just make sure to grab a 4.7kΩ pull-up resistor if you go with the 4-pin version.

**Placement question:**
The DHT11 sensor sits inside the tent at plant level, the soil moisture sensor goes into the soil, and the Arduino with the WiFi module stays outside the tent near the top where it's accessible. The sensor wires run down through a cable gland into the tent to reach the DHT11 and soil sensor. The main constraint is WiFi range—if the ESP-01 needs to transmit from inside the tent, the signal might struggle depending on the tent material and distance from the router.

But there's a simpler option: the Arduino could push data to ThingSpeak via USB connected to a basic computer or an old router running custom firmware, avoiding WiFi complications entirely. Or if you go with the ESP-01, you could mount it outside the tent with an antenna for better range. I should ask what specific WiFi solution they're considering before finalizing the placement.

On the buying decision—definitely hold off until the architecture is locked down. Getting the position and connectivity approach sorted first means they won't waste money on parts that don't fit the final design.

messages.content:
ok, I don't find the same I already have at the store I am looking at right now, but I found this one, the AHT10, https://www.mechatronicstore.cl/modulo-sensor-de-temperatura-y-humedad-digital-aht10/. Is this a reliable option we could try?

How can I search for the dht11 with built-in module? Does it have a specific name?

I agree on position everything inside but power. We are working in a tent with 80cm x 80 cm x 120cm (height). The pot is 11 Liters. I think I may add more pots with other plants I want to grow, such as fresh chives, coriander, and maybe bigger plants I want to move outside later. It's been hard to grow plants outside so I think I can use the indoor as germination box too, for plants that I can move outside later on. So having the arduino inside to improve communication to sensors is crucial for proper environment control.

## AHT10 — yes, it's actually better

The AHT10 is a solid upgrade over the DHT11:

| | DHT11 | AHT10 |
|--|-------|-------|
| Communication | Single-wire (timing critical) | **I2C** (more stable) |
| Accuracy | ±2°C / ±5% RH | ±0.3°C / ±2% RH |
| Library support | DHTlib / Adafruit | Adafruit AHTX0 |
| Pin count | 1 data pin | 2 pins (SCL, SDA) |
| Size | Larger | Smaller |
| Price | ~$1-2 | ~$2-3 |

**I2C is more reliable** — it handles cable length better, no timing issues, and allows multiple I2C devices on the same 2 wires. Downside: needs 2 pins instead of 1, but the Nano has plenty.

**Code change:** Just load the AHT10 library instead of DHT — swap the library call and sensor initialization, rest of the ThingSpeak logic stays the same.

**Verdict:** Buy the AHT10. It's worth the extra dollar.

---

## DHT11 module naming

Search on the store for:
- "DHT11 module"
- "DHT11 con placa" (with board)
- "DHT11 sensor module"

Look for the 3-pin version (VCC, GND, DATA) — that means it has the built-in pull-up resistor and signal conditioning. Avoid the 4-pin standalone sensor unless you want to add your own resistors.

---

## Layout inside your tent (80×80×120cm)

For your tent dimensions, here's the placement:

```
TOP (on tent rim / hanging):
├── Arduino Nano + ESP-01 in enclosure
├── USB power from outside
└── Cable glands for sensor wires

MIDDLE (~40-50cm down):
└── DHT11 (at plant canopy level)

BOTTOM (soil surface ~5-10cm from bottom):
└── Soil moisture sensor (in pot)
```

**Distances from Arduino to sensors:**
- DHT11: ~40-50cm
- Soil sensor: ~45-50cm

Both are fine for I2C/analog — well within normal cable lengths.

**On expansion (chives, coriander, future pots):**
The capacitive soil sensor is a single-point probe — it only reads the pot it's in. For multiple pots you'd need:
- Multiple sensors (one per pot) on different analog pins
- Or accept that you're monitoring the main plant's soil as a proxy

**Suggestion:** Print multiple probe holders so you can move the single sensor between pots if needed. Or buy a second sensor later when you add more pots.

---

**Recommendation for the store:**
- Skip DHT11 entirely → get **AHT10** (better accuracy, I2C = more stable)
- Get **2× AHT10** — one for inside tent, one spare
- Keep your existing DHT11 3-pin module as a backup

Want me to update the shopping list in smartplant-v2.md with the AHT10 instead of DHT11?

**AHT10 vs DHT11:**
The AHT10 is actually more modern and better than the DHT11 in several ways:
- I2C communication (not single-wire like DHT11) — more stable, no timing issues
- More accurate and consistent readings
- Smaller form factor
- Better temperature and humidity range

The downside: it uses I2C, so it needs 2 pins (SCL, SDA) instead of 1. 

The AHT10 communicates via I2C while the DHT11 uses a single-wire protocol, so the wiring approach differs but both are digital. I should check what libraries the existing codebase uses to see if switching would require code changes. On the search term front, I'm realizing the naming isn't standardized — most vendors just list "DHT11 module" or "DHT11 with PCB" rather than having a consistent technical name, so searching by those common terms is probably the best approach. I'm realizing the AHT10 would be a solid upgrade—it communicates over I2C which is more reliable than the DHT11's single-wire protocol, and it's smaller and more accurate. Since it speaks I2C rather than the DHT11's proprietary timing-based protocol, the code would need different libraries and pin configuration, but the user would just define the I2C address in their sketch rather than specifying a single data pin.

For the tent setup, I'm sketching out the physical layout: an 80x80 base with a 120cm height means the Arduino would sit on the rim at the top, with the DHT11 sensor positioned at plant level around 40-50cm down, and the soil moisture probe at the substrate surface. The distances are manageable—roughly 40-50cm for the DHT11 and about 45cm for the soil sensor from the microcontroller.

For wiring, I'd route sensor cables through cable glands at the top, with the USB power running from outside the tent to keep heat sources minimal. Since there's room for expansion with additional pots, I should factor in extra cable length and plan for multiple sensor units if the user wants to monitor more plants later.

messages.content: Ok, just for clarification Rent to Francisca (50% of $1,100,000) | $500,000, $500000 is not 50% of %1100000 but lest. I have never calculated the exact % but because Francisca earns around $4200000 and I $3100000 we decided to split it this way. Could you calculate the exact % and tell me if the way we are spliting is in inline with how much we earn?

Quick math:

**Household income:**
- Francisca: $4,200,000
- O'car: $3,100,000
- **Total: $7,300,000**

**Proportional split (by income):**
- Francisca: 57.53%
- O'car: 42.47%

**What you actually pay:**
- Francisca pays landlord: $600,000 (54.55%)
- O'car pays Francisca: $500,000 (45.45%)

**Difference:**
| | Proportional | Actual | Gap |
|--|--|--|--|
| O'car | $467,170 | $500,000 | +$32,830 (+3%) |
| Francisca | $632,830 | $600,000 | -$32,830 (-3%) |

**Verdict:** You're paying slightly more than your proportional share (+3%), Francisca is paying slightly less (-3%). It's a minor deviation — close enough that it's reasonable, but if you want to be precise, O'car could pay $467,170 instead of $500,000 (~$33k difference).

The discrepancy is small enough that it's probably not worth renegotiating — the $33k/mo difference is ~3% of your rent contribution. The arrangement is broadly in line with income proportions.

messages.content: good, thanks. I've noticed that at the csv files you are using commas for currency separation, but that will break our csv file. I think we need to update our convention on how to create csv files, I would rather use ; than , for column separation, then you can use comma for currency separation. What do you think? Should we update our readme at finance and then update our csv files and scripts if there are?

Agreed — comma in currency + comma as delimiter is a guaranteed break. Semicolon as delimiter is the right call, especially for Chilean/European number formatting.

Now I understand the issue. The script uses `csv.QUOTE_ALL` which quotes every field with double quotes. This means the amount field would be written as `"$255,288"` instead of `$255,288`. However, the problem is that even with quotes, the comma in the amount is inside the field. But with proper CSV quoting, this should actually be fine.

Wait, the user says "I notice that at the csv files you are using commas for currency separation". Looking at the actual CSV file I wrote:
```
2026-03-31,Buffer,Prusa MK4 → MK4S upgrade parts,$255,288,MercadoPago,Several parts for upgrade,April,2026


```

I see the real problem now — the dollar sign is included in the amount field itself, and since it's not quoted, the comma in `$255,288` breaks the CSV structure. The script uses QUOTE_ALL which should quote every field, but it looks like the amount is being written without the dollar sign prefix, which is why the comma inside the number is causing parsing issues.

The core solution is switching to semicolons as the delimiter so commas can be used freely in currency formatting without any ambiguity. I need to update the README to reflect the new convention, modify the CSV script to use semicolons instead, and then fix the existing CSV files accordingly.

```

messages.content: ok, good, back on the arduino, care to explain what the mini560 regulator is for?

The Mini560 is a **DC-DC buck converter** that steps voltage down from 5V to a stable 3.3V.

**Why it matters for ESP-01:**

The ESP-01 WiFi module runs on 3.3V — not 5V. If you power it directly from the Nano's 5V pin, you're already at the edge of spec. The problem is:

- USB power can be 4.5V–5.5V depending on the charger quality
- The ESP-01 WiFi radio draws spikes of current (~200mA) when transmitting
- These spikes cause voltage sag — if the supply can't deliver, the ESP browns out or reboots randomly

The Mini560 sits between 5V and the ESP-01, giving you clean, stable 3.3V even when the input fluctuates.

**Do you need it?**

Probably not if:
- Your 5V supply is clean (a good phone charger or dedicated 5V 2A adapter)
- You flash the ESP with WiFi retry logic (it reconnects automatically on brownout)

Probably yes if:
- You're seeing random ESP disconnections
- Your power source is long-distance USB cable or a noisy hub

**Practical recommendation:** Leave it on the optional list. Get a good 5V 2A adapter, test first, add the Mini560 only if the ESP is unstable. It's cheap ($1) and small — easy to add later.

Same principle applies to the perfboard: build the harness on dupont/protoboard first, test everything, then solder permanently if it's stable.

messages.content:
ok, our solution should consider the converter because the house we are at is old and voltage comes and goes. Better safe than sorry if we aim to trust the device.

Will we use the perfboard for soldering or for testing only? I already have a protoboard I can use.

Good call on the Mini560 — unstable voltage + ESP-01 is a guaranteed headache. It's in the final build.

**On perfboard vs protoboard:**

The smartplant-v2 plan was soldered perfboard for a permanent harness. But since you have a protoboard:

**Option A — Protoboard as permanent:**
- Pros: Reusable, no soldering, easy to modify
- Cons: Springs can loosen from vibration (earthquakes/movement), not as clean inside the enclosure

**Option B — Protoboard for testing, soldered perfboard for final:**
- Pros: You validate everything works before committing, then build a clean permanent version
- Cons: Double the work

**Option C — Skip perfboard entirely, solder directly:**
- Pros: Simplest, smallest footprint inside enclosure
- Cons: Harder to modify later

For your use case (permanent indoor tent, some vibration from fans/location), I'd recommend **Option B** — use your protoboard now to validate the circuit works, then solder a permanent perfboard for the final enclosure. The protoboard becomes your bench tester for future projects.

Want me to update smartplant-v2.md to clarify the perfboard approach and note that you have a protoboard available for testing?

messages.content:
My mistake, good catch, we are using arduino nano v3.0 CH340 compatible.

Problem with Arduino UNO is that the board is bigger and has too many ports for what we need. I have found a box size 100x68x50mm (https://www.mechatronicstore.cl/caja-plastica-para-proyectos-blanca/) $3090. If we use the arduino nano can everything fit here? 

I haven't found perf board on the page I am looking at. Maybe I should export the shopping chart and share it with you so you can help me find alternative stores later on, what do you think?

100x68x50mm is tight but doable for a Nano-based build. Quick layout check:

**Components to fit:**
- Nano Nano v3 (with headers): ~45 × 18mm
- ESP-01: ~14 × 15mm
- Mini560: ~20 × 10mm
- AHT10 module: ~15 × 12mm
- Protoboard/terminals for connections

**Fits:** Yes, but tight. The height (50mm) is the constraint — the Nano with ESP-01 stacked is ~25-30mm, leaving ~20mm for wiring and cable glands. Route wires flat, use right-angle connectors, and position the Nano against one wall with components arranged around it rather than stacked.

**Cable glands:** 3 glands on the short end (68mm wide) — you'll need to drill or use pre-threaded holes. 3× PG-7 glands need ~9mm holes each, spaced ~15mm apart. That fits on the 100mm side.

---

On the shopping chart — yes, export it and send it to me. I can help cross-reference parts across multiple stores, check prices, and flag anything that's overpriced or incompatible. Better to map it out before buying.
messages.reasoning:
The user wants to know if the Arduino Nano + ESP-01 + AHT10 + capacitive soil sensor + Mini560 + wiring can all fit in a 100x68x50mm box. Let me think about the component sizes:

- Arduino Nano v3: ~45 x 18mm (with headers)
- ESP-01: ~14 x 15mm
- Mini560 DC-DC module: ~20 x 10mm
- AHT10 module: ~15 x 12mm (small I2C module)
- JST connectors: small 2-3 pin headers
- Some wiring and heat shrink

100x68x50mm = 100mm long, 68mm wide, 50mm deep. 

At 100 x 68mm, that's actually a decent footprint for a Nano-based project. The challenge isn't the horizontal space—the real issue is height. With the Nano's headers and any shield on top, I'm probably looking at 15-20mm of vertical clearance, and with the ESP-01 stacked on top of that, I need to account for how they'll physically layer within the 50mm depth constraint.

The bigger concern is wire routing and cable glands. With three glands for the AHT10, soil sensor, and power, I need to figure out where they actually mount on the box and how much space that leaves for components. A 100x68x50mm enclosure feels tight but potentially workable for a Nano setup, though I'd want to actually verify the component placement before committing to it.

messages.content: In regards of box sizes there are other sizes too, 158x90x60 200x120x75

The 158x90x60 is the sweet spot — comfortable without being oversized:

| Box | Internal space | Verdict |
|-----|---------------|---------|
| 100x68x50 | Tight, 50mm height limits stacking | Usable but cramped |
| **158x90x60** | Plenty of room, 60mm height | **Recommended** |
| 200x120x75 | Oversized for this build | Waste of space/money |

For ~$3,090 (same price range), the 158x90x60 gives you:
- Easy Nano + ESP-01 placement
- Room for cable management
- 3 cable glands with comfortable spacing
- Space to add a small perfboard later

The 100x68x50 works but you'd be squeezing every mm. If the bigger one is only slightly more expensive, go with 158x90x60.

What are the prices for the larger boxes? And send the full shopping list when you're ready — I'll help cross-check.

{"content": "     1|# SmartPlant v2 — Robust Arduino Monitoring Station
     2|
     3|**Date:** 04/04/2026
     4|**Status:** Planning
     5|
     6|## Objective
     7|Build a robust, permanent Arduino-based monitoring station for the Lemon Haze grow to replace the current prototype that suffers from cable disconnections during earthquakes/movement.
     8|
     9|## Reference Design
    10|**Robust Arduino Nano Enclosure**
    11|- URL: https://cults3d.com/en/3d-model/tool/robust-arduino-nano-enclosure

[Replying to: "hold my chart right there as I will browse the missing parts in other websites. I am checking arduino.cl, maybe you could check for me? we need a perfboard 10x10 would be ok no? we need heat shrinking tube, but how much? Also in regard of the cable I could use RJ45 cable no? If I unstrip it, wouldn't it be better to use rj45 over paralel awg 22?

I could try sending you the picture of the shopping chart again so you see what we have at https://www.mechatronicstore.cl/"]

You were over than hour processing something. i sent you this and it took you so long that i restarted the gateway

Sorry about that — the gateway was struggling. Let me address your questions now:

## RJ45 vs Silicone Wire

RJ45 (ethernet cable) is actually a **mixed bag** for this application:

**RJ45 solid-core pros:**
- 8 conductors — plenty for multiple sensors
- Cheap, easy to source
- RJ45 connectors for clean detach

**RJ45 solid-core cons:**
- **Solid core** = stiff, not flexible
- Not designed for vibrating/moving environments
- Overkill for 2 sensors with 4-6 wires total
- Fan vibration inside tent + door movement = broken wires eventually

**Silicone wire** (what we listed) is stranded, bends thousands of times without breaking. For your earthquake-prone setup, **silicone wins**. RJ45 is better for fixed house wiring, not inside a tent with fans and vibration.

**Compromise:** Use silicone wire inside the tent, but terminate with **JST connectors** (which you already have on the list) for clean disconnection. That's the strain-relief approach.

## Heat Shrink

1 meter is plenty. Get 3:1 ratio, 3-5mm diameter range (covers most 22 AWG wire junctions). You have 3-4 junctions, each needing ~2-3cm coverage.

## Perfboard size

10×10cm is fine. The Nano is ~4.5×1.8cm, you'll have room to spare. 5×7cm also works if you want tighter.

---

messages.reasoning:
The user is saying I was very slow responding over an hour ago. They're asking about:

1. Arduino.cl - checking for perfboard and heat shrink
2. Perfboard size: 10x10 is fine for the Nano build
3. Heat shrink tubing: how much?
4. RJ45 cable vs parallel AWG22 for sensor wiring

Let me help them with these questions now without the delay. I should address each point concisely.

RJ45 (ethernet cable) is actually a good idea for sensor wiring:
- Solid copper core, good for fixed installations
- 8 conductors = multiple sensors or plenty of spares
- Already shielded in some cases
- Easy to terminate with RJ45 plugs


- However, for the I2C bus (AHT10) and analog sensor (soil moisture), you need specific wiring: I2C uses 4 wires minimum (VCC, GND, SCL, SDA), and the soil sensor needs 3 wires (VCC, GND, signal), so RJ45's 8 conductors would be overkill but workable.

The real issue is that RJ45 uses 22-24 AWG solid core ethernet cable, which is rigid and not ideal for the tent environment where vibration and movement are concerns. For the short runs needed (40-50cm inside the tent), flexible stranded wire would be better than the stiff solid core ethernet cable, so the user's silicone wire recommendation is actually the more practical choice despite RJ45 being convenient.

For heat shrink, a 1-meter roll of 3:1 ratio tubing in the 3-6mm range should handle most connections, and a 10x10cm perfboard will work fine for the Nano build. Now I should look up the perfboard and heat shrink options on arduino.cl to see what's actually available.

**Heat shrink tubing** — no results for "termoencogible", "heat shrink", or "manguito termocontraible". You'll need to source this elsewhere:
- MercadoLibre (search "termoencogible" or "manguito termocontraible")
- Local electronics shops
- Should be cheap — ~$1-2 per meter

## Summary for arduino.cl cart:
| Item | Price |
|------|-------|
| Tarjeta Perforada 10×10 cm | $4,990 |

---

**On RJ45 vs silicone wire:** Skip the RJ45. Solid-core ethernet is too stiff for inside the tent. Silicone stranded wire is the right call for vibration resistance. JST connectors on the sensor cables give you the detachability you want without the rigidity problems of RJ45.

**Heat shrink:** 1 meter of 3:1 ratio, 3-5mm diameter. Grab it from MercadoLibre or a local electronics shop — it's cheap everywhere.

The Afel AWG options are all **30 AWG** — that's actually **thinner than 22 AWG**, not better.

**AWG comparison:**
| AWG | Cross-section | Use case |
|-----|-------------|----------|
| 30 AWG | 0.05mm² | Very thin — signal wires, fine electronics |
| **22 AWG** | 0.33mm² | **Our target — durable power/signal** |
| 20 AWG | 0.52mm² | Thicker — higher current |

**AWG numbers work inversely** — larger number = thinner wire.

For your project:
- 30 AWG is too thin for soil sensor analog signal runs and any meaningful current
- 22 AWG is the right balance of flexibility + durability for 50cm cable runs inside a vibrating tent
- You only need ~1m of actual wire, so 190m rolls at Afel are overkill unless you have other projects

**Verdict:** Skip the Afel AWG rolls. They're cheap per meter but wrong gauge for this application. 

For silicone wire:
- Check **MercadoLibre** for "cable silicona 22 AWG" — usually sold by the meter or in small spools
- Local electronics shops also typically carry it in small lengths

For 1 meter of 22 AWG silicone wire you're probably looking at under $1,000 CLP anywhere.

What about mechatronicstore.cl — do they have silicone wire or regular stranded 22 AWG wire? That could consolidate more of your order in one place.

- 30 AWG = 0.0507mm² cross-section
- 22 AWG = 0.3255mm² cross-section

22 AWG is about 6x thicker than 30 AWG. For sensor wires carrying current over ~50cm inside a tent, 30 AWG might be too thin for the soil sensor analog signal and any power delivery. 22 AWG is the right call for durability and conductivity.

**$11,990 for 100m of 22 AWG stranded parallel cable** (Rojo/Negro) — that's $120 per meter, which is a fair price for stranded wire in Chile.

**Key specs:**
- 22 AWG ✅
- **Multifilar (Stranded)** ✅ — good flexibility
- PVC insulation — standard, not silicone, but stranded construction handles vibration better than solid core
- Rated 80°C, up to 7A — more than enough for this project

**Trade-off:** It's PVC, not silicone. Silicone is more flexible and handles vibration better long-term. But for 50cm fixed cable runs inside the tent that won't be flexed repeatedly, **PVC stranded is perfectly adequate**.

**One thing:** It's **parallel cable** (two conductors bonded together, red+black). You'll strip and separate the two conductors for your runs — easy enough.

**Verdict:** Good buy. At $11,990 for 100m you have wire for this project plus plenty left over for future projects. Split the cost with Francisca if you want, or just keep the roll.

You still need to source: cable glands PG-7, heat shrink, and JST connectors — check mechatronicstore for those or hit MercadoLibre.

Want me to search mechatronicstore for cable glands and heat shrink while you continue shopping?
messages.reasoning:
I can see the product details:- **Price:** $11,990 IVA incluido
- **100 meters of cable paralelo AWG 22** (Rojo/Negro)- **Multifilar (Stranded)** — good, flexible- **PVC insulation** — not silicone, but stranded is still flexible enough for this use case
- **22 AWG** — correct gauge- **Per two-conductor parallel cable** (Rojo/Negro)

Key specs:- 0.326 mm² cross-section- Up to 7A for short runs- 80°C max temp- PVC insulation (not silicone, but that's mainly for high-temp environments)

This is a good option. It's parallel (two conductors side by side), red and black. It's stranded, which is good. PVC insulation is standard — silicone would be more flexible but PVC is fine for inside a tent.

The price of $11,990 for 100m of 22 AWG stranded wire is very reasonable. For comparison, 100m of genuine silicone wire would likely cost significantly more.

However, it's **parallel PVC cable**, not silicone. For inside the tent with vibration:
- PVC is stiffer than silicone — could crack over time in vibrating environment
- But for 50cm cable runs that don't move much, it should be fine
- The stranded construction helps

At $11,990 for 100m, it's very cheap. You only need ~1m, so it's a lot of excess, but it's the only option available.

The user could also consider: cut just 1-2 meters from the roll for their project and use the rest elsewhere. 

Since the parallel cable has both conductors combined in one jacket, I'd need to separate them for individual runs — one conductor for signal and another for power/ground. This works fine for the project.

For the temperature side: tent environments typically stay around 25-30°C with some heat from grow lights, well within the 80°C rating. The real concern is mechanical flexibility — PVC isn't ideal for repeated vibration, but at 50cm fixed runs, it should hold up fine. Silicone would be better long-term, but at nearly $12k for 100m, this PVC cable is a practical short-term solution.

The price breaks down to roughly $120 per meter, which is reasonable for stranded 22 AWG wire in Chile. For just the 1-2 meters needed, it's economical to buy the whole roll rather than source smaller quantities elsewhere.

messages.reasoning:
The user is asking about the ESP-12F (or ESP-12E) module instead of the ESP-01S. Let me think about this.

ESP-01S vs ESP-12F/E:
- ESP-01S: Small, 2 GPIO pins exposed, comes with PCB antenna, harder to breadboard, 4MB flash typical
- ESP-12F/E: More GPIO pins (4-6), larger, metal shield included, better RF performance, needs breadboard adapter to use on protoboard

For this project:
- ESP-01S: What we're buying, 2 GPIO pins might be enough (TX, RX for serial + maybe 1-2 extra)
- ESP-12F/E: More pins, but requires adapter for protoboard use


- The ESP-01S is actually the better choice here since it's more compact and comes as a complete module ready to go. With the ESP-01S, we get CH_PD and RST which can be tied high, GPIO0 and GPIO2 for basic status LEDs, and the UART pins for flashing. The ESP-12F would give us more GPIO options but at the cost of additional adapter hardware we don't need for this straightforward sensor project.

The **ESP-12** is a different module — not a direct replacement. Here's the comparison:

| | ESP-01S (what we're buying) | ESP-12 |
|--|---|---|
| GPIO pins | 2 | up to 17 |
| ADC | No | Yes (1 channel) |
| Antenna | PCB trace | PCB integrated |
| Breadboard friendly | ✅ Ready to use | ❌ Needs adapter |
| Price | $2,790 (mechastore) | $3,700 (afel) |

**The key point:** In our architecture, the **Nano handles all sensor reading** and passes data to the ESP-01S over UART (TX/RX). The ESP-01S only needs to forward WiFi data — it doesn't need extra GPIO.

The ESP-12 would be relevant if you wanted the ESP to read sensors directly (no Nano), which would require more complex firmware and is a bigger project.

**Also:** ESP-12 needs a separate adapter board to use on protoboard — that adds cost and complexity.

**Our setup (Nano + ESP-01S)** is the right architecture for this project. Simpler firmware, already buying the ESP-01S, and the Nano handles the heavy lifting.

Now — the ESP-01S you already have in your mechatronicstore cart is correct. If you want to swap for ESP-12 at Afel you'd need to buy adapter boards too, making it more expensive overall.
messages.reasoning:
So the ESP-12 (which is likely the ESP-12E or ESP-12F) is $3,700 at Afel, compared to the ESP-01S which is $2,790 at Mechatronicstore. 

Key differences:
- ESP-01S: 2 GPIO exposed, no ADC, compact, comes on a carrier board
- ESP-12: Up to 17 GPIO, 1 ADC, needs adapter to use on breadboard/protoboard

For our project, we're using the ESP-01S with the Arduino Nano. The Arduino Nano handles the sensor reading and passes data to the ESP-01S over UART (TX/RX). The ESP-01S just needs to forward data to WiFi. 

With this architecture, we're really only using the ESP-01S for its radio functionality, so the ESP-12's extra GPIO and ADC are redundant. The ESP-01S is more compact and cost less at $2,790 versus $3,700, plus we'd need a separate adapter board for the ESP-12 anyway, making it pricier overall. For this project, the simpler approach makes sense.
